data.title: Medical Properties Trust: A Turnaround Story I'm Not Buying Ahead Of Earnings
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Timberica Yield as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment reasoning

The investment portfolio of Medical Properties Trust (NYSE:MPW) has deteriorated in value over a 4 year period. In January news broke that one of the most significant tenants to MPW is filing for Chapter 11 Bankruptcy after defaulting on rent payments since mid-2024.

The tenant has been a thorn in the side of MPW and crippled the stock price. MPW is now a hopeful turnaround story but one displaying considerable risk including a large debt position and a premium share price in comparison to the actual intrinsic value. MPW is due to report its fourth quarterly results on Thursday 27/2 and given the current state of the business little news can bring my bearish view to a neutral one.

Company Description

“We acquire and develop healthcare facilities and lease the facilities to healthcare operating companies under long-term net leases, which require the tenant to bear most of the costs associated with the property. The majority of our leased assets are owned 100%; however, we do own some leased assets through joint ventures with other partners that share our view that healthcare facilities are part of the infrastructure of any community, which we refer to as investments in unconsolidated real estate joint ventures. We also make mortgage loans to healthcare operators collateralized by their real estate assets.”

Source: MPW 10Q Filed November 12, 2024

Implied impact of Chapter 11 on MPW stock

Since January 13 shares of MPW stock have risen from $3.91 to $4.95 as the market reacted positively to news of Prospect Medical Group filing for Chapter 11 bankruptcy after years of difficulties with liquidity levels. The latest rent payment to MPW from Prospect goes back to Q2 2024, it has since then defaulted on each due payment.

The initial investments MPW made were valued at $1.5 billion in 2019 for 14 acute care hospitals. Challenges for Prospects was initially sparked by the outbreak of the Coronavirus. It could up until Q4 2022 make rent payments on time. Prospect failed however to reach agreements with third parties for refinancing which would have covered future rent payments, MPW was forced to record a $280 million impairment charge. The underlying value of these properties was reduced by $170 million as a result.

The risk with the restructuring following the filing is that MPW might receive lower rates from Prospect in order for the company to maintain a healthy balance sheet and not default again on payments. This would of course impact operating income for MPW which is why investors initially turned their back on MPW and the share price fell.

The newfound optimism for MPW I believe can quickly turn to pessimism if MPW can’t quickly realize higher operating income. Based on my model shares aren't that cheap despite the fall since mid-2022. Financing future dividends will prove challenging for MPW unless underlying assets begin to perform. I expect further asset writedowns in Q4 which will negatively impact NAV, further supporting why a bearish view is justified now.

Can MPT secure enough financing

MPW commented on the Chapter 11 filing by citing how they remain focused on protecting their initial investments from 2019, valued now at $1.7 billion.

“Prospect’s California facilities have reported volume growth and improved coverage trends in 2024, its overall liquidity has been adversely impacted by stalled sales processes across various East Coast markets, including Rhode Island where MPT has no interest in the real estate.”

Source: MPW Press Release

2 days after filing for Chapter 11 Prospects released a press release stating that it had received approval from the US Bankruptcy Court to access new money term loans, $100 million principal, and $29 million accessible immediately. The term loans are debtor-in-possession. To MPW this was a negative development as it meant that it would likely receive compensation later due to MPW now since a DIP approval was made. This slows down the time that MPW can receive the money.

MPW currently holds 177 properties which are general acute care hospitals. In California MPW has 13 current asset types like these. Dueing 2019 14 assets like these were acquired when investing in Prospects, currently 11 of them are located in California. If we keep in mind that this asset class performed the best during Q3 2024 at 2.7x it's reasonable to assume that MPT has the foundation to operationally get back on track but also in due time pay back outstanding rents owed to MPW.

With this information, I am making the case that MPT noise is just that, short-term noise, and over the next 12 - 24 months the current situation should be resolved and leave marginal impacts on MPW portfolio value. In fact, market assumptions are for a top-line recovery in fiscal years 2026 and 2027.

Underlying assets are showing early signs of poor performance

MPWs current asset portfolio is divided into four main asset types - General Acute Care Hospitals - Behavioral Health facilities - Post Acute Care Facilities - Freestanding ER/Urgent Care Facilities.

Total properties were 402, 177 being in the first asset type and 133 in the third. Total asset value was $15.2 billion as of September 30, 2024. Given the negative change in asset value that occurred during 2022 for Prospect, I expect to see the total asset value decline once again in Q4 2024. Total assets have declined 3.61% each year on average since 2020. Despite groups 1 and 3 being close in the number of properties, General Acute Care Hospitals make up 61.1% of all asset value and were responsible for 61.6% of all revenues.

MPW has a well-diversified medical property portfolio where no individual facility makes up more than 2% of the total portfolio value. The two largest are Healthcare Systems of America and Circle Health.

2024 asset sales

During 2024 MPW sold several assets. In April MPW sold a majority interest in Utah hospitals which generated $1.1 billion of total cash proceeds. MPW retained a 25% stake and the buyer placed a new-recourse secured financing netting MPW with $190 million. All cash proceeds will be utilized to pay down long-term debts, notably a $300 million Australian term loan.

In July MPW went on to sell more assets, receiving $160 million for the sale of a 50-bed Arizona General Hospital. Much like the April asset sale, all proceeds will be used toward paying down long-term debts.

Asset sales like these further reduce the operating income for MPW and with the share price instead rising the actual NAV per share has deceased during 2024 according to my models.

Between Q3 2024 and Q3 2023 revenues derived from rents declined from $229 million to $169 million. The problem with the state of MPW is that interest expenses remain the same whilst revenue-producing assets are declining.

The net income was negative due to $607 million in real estate impairment charges. $425 million of this was due to a previous investment in an asset-backed credit facility from Steward’s.

Since MPWs ability to generate a profit from its assets plummeted in 2022 the stock price followed a similar trajectory. TTM ROA is negative 17.47% and nothing points to a rapid recovery given the circumstances with Prospects and the loss on rental revenues there.

Since then FFO has plummeted putting the dividend payments in jeopardy. TTM is negative $1.8 billion. During 2023 MPW recorded $287 million of positive FFO. At $0.08 quarterly dividend payments, the annual payout would be $191 million based on 599 million common shares. If projections become reality in 2025 and MPW nets $0.67 FFO per share it can cover the current dividend with a payout ratio of 47%. What worries me is that MPW won't see the recovery the market is hoping for. The healthcare sector reached record defaults in 2024 which led to several downgrades from credit rators. The one struggling asset type in the portfolio has been Post Acute Care Facilities. It hasn't posted the same rent coverage growth as its others. It's for me the first sign of a potentially struggling investment portfolio in 2025.

Total debts will be a large hurdle to overcome

Total debt value has ballooned up to $9.2 billion. To counter this and rapidly pay down debt to deleverage MPWs current balance the company announced its intention to raise $2 billion in senior secured notes due 2032. The offering comes with €500 million in aggregate principal notes.

“with the intent to allocate the net proceeds towards redeeming its existing senior notes maturing in 2025 and 2026, and to address other general corporate purposes.”

Source: MPW Press Release

The amount has since changed to $1.5 billion in notes due 2032 at 8.% and €1 billion in principle notes due 2032 at 7%. This changed the total offering to $2.54 billion.

The current debt profile is in light terms a bit scrambled. The priced offering is intended to address 2025 and 2026 obligations, valued at $2.2 billion more or less equally distributed between the years. The offering caused a rating upgrade by Moodys to B3 and the offering of a B2 and higher share price soon followed too.

“A rating downgrade is likely if the REIT's liquidity weakens or if or if it faces additional tenant challenges within its portfolio.”

Source: Moody Press Release

The primary concern for investors should be that MPW actually manages to raise liquidity within the business and rent coverage remains at a high level across all asset types. Moody cites that if this is not realized a potential rating downgrade is possible. We saw how markets reacted to an upgrade, a downgrade will have the opposite effect most likely. As the saying goes, “The stock market takes the stairs up, but the elevator down”.

“However, the materially higher interest cost for the new debt would weaken the REIT's fixed coverage ratio and its unencumbered asset ratio”

Source: Moody Press Release

The press release went on to include how MPW is issuing notes at a time of distress and challenges as the received rate is significantly higher than what was for anything in the debt profile prior. It's sort of like pushing the issue in front of you and MPW will have to deal with these higher interest payments. This puts further pressure on an already weak bottom line. The rating upgrade did not mean MPW raised the dividend but rather kept it the same as in the previous quarter, $0.08 per share. A raise could have signaled management confidence in increasing future liquidity, but remaining cautious in a difficult market environment is a reasonable approach in my view.

Short coverage sets up MPWs for a short-term price squeeze

According to Fintel, the short interest ratio is 39 days to cover with 215 million shares being shorted currently. The short interest float is 43.69%. Since MPW is due to report earnings this weak a positive beat and upbeat outlook on liquidity levels and crucially operating income could lift the share price.

Depending on the outcome of the report I can see shares lifting between 15 - 20% over the next 2 months, similar to what happened between now and January when news came out about Prospects. I won't change the rating based on this finding as I don't “play earnings” when I invest.

Combined valuation models display significant short-term risks should the turnaround begin to crack

With an NAV model, the intrinsic value per share comes out to a negative $10 due to falling operating income, with the expectation this will continue in 2025. A cap rate of 9%, above the industry average due to poor asset performance on MPW's part. Debt assumptions are $7 billion which includes the recently issued senior notes target LT debt repayments.

A DDM reveals an intrinsic value of $4.4 per share, leaving an 11.4% premium. The model assumes a negative 0.5% dividend growth rate due to difficult market conditions and MPWs need to sell assets to pay down debt further reducing the likelihood of FFO increasing. Given this, I am applying a 12% WACC to reflect these risks.

The REIT Average Multiple Model is the only one that returns a higher intrinsic value than where MPW currently trades. The implied value is $7.61 per share based on an average P/AFFO multiple of 19 between four peers.

Combing all the outcomes for finding the intrinsic value the average is $1.04 per share. The reason for the low number is that the negative NAV brings down the average by a lot. The average between the DDM and AMM models is $6.35 per share. I believe it's important to account for the financial state of the business and give the evident risks of the high net debt I choose to not buy shares in MPW in its current state.

Risks

Debt is a large risk to MPW. It has crippled the company's ability to invest in new properties and drive revenue growth through rent payments. MPW is in a state where before it can return to a growth phase it needs to tackle its debts aggressively. This means that investors risk getting lower capital returns as MPW sees it more necessary to divert that to repayments of debts.

A period of higher interest rates had detrimental effects on some of the tenants for MPW, so re-entering such a period would be a risk for MPW. The safety of the asset portfolio would deteriorate if tenants can't reliably pay rent on time.

Since my thesis is bearish, the upside risk is that MPW beats out expectations in the next report and the stock rallies. Given the state of the company and market going into the next report, I am still confident in my rating.

Final thoughts

My thoughts on MPW are that the business has been in terminal decline since challenges began to arise in 2022. It holds a vast investment portfolio but performance across the board is beginning to crack. The trend during 2024 for MPW was to sell assets to accelerate LT debt repayments. The implied intrinsic value is far lower than the current share price due to the high debt levels. I don't see the problem being resolved in the short term. The poor state of the company means little news during the upcoming report could bring me to make a neutral rating, less so a bullish one. Shares are a sell.

data.title: NXG: For The Next Generation Of Income Investors
data.description:
One of the many forecasts for future energy infrastructure demands that I found during my research for this investment fund predicts that considerable additional investment is needed in both the transmission and distribution of electrical energy over the next decade. According to the World Economic Forum, investments in power grids between now and 2030 need to double to meet the growing demand:

  • The energy transition requires the upgrading of the entire energy value chain, including transmission and distribution.

Over the last decade, investment in power grids globally amounted to $300-400 billion per year. The International Energy Agency (IEA) believes this should double by 2030. Indeed, the world needs to expand its power grid infrastructure, as growing electrification, integration of variable renewable energy, and adaptation to climate change all require larger and more modern electricity networks. Significant investment is needed in transmission infrastructure to connect new power generation to grids, as well as to support system-wide electrification of all sectors including electric vehicles and heat pumps. At the same time, existing, often ageing, infrastructure will need to be upgraded.

Some of the companies that stand to benefit from this trend of increasing demand include Vistra Corporation (VST), Constellation Energy (CEG), and Energy Transfer (ET). Both VST and CEG topped the S&P 500 leaderboard at the end of 2024 as reported on January 2, 2025:

Vistra finished the first trading day of the new year as the top gainer on the S&P 500, +8.5%, after racking up a scorching 258% increase for 2024, which made it the year's second strongest S&P performer.

Vistra led a group of nuclear power plant operators in 2024, including Constellation Energy, which surged 91% for the year, and NRG Energy (NRG), which climbed 74%, with gains more closely resembling the high-growth tech sector than the typically defensive play of utilities; on Thursday, Constellation and NRG added another +8.4% and +2.8%, respectively.

Constellation Energy said Thursday it was awarded $1B in contracts to supply nuclear power to the U.S. government over the next decade, delivering electricity to more than 13 federal agencies as part of the agreements with the General Services Administration.

Likewise, Energy Transfer has inked some big contracts in recent months, including a 20-year LNG supply deal with Chevron as well as a long-term natural gas supply agreement with a Texas Data Center. ET had a stellar year in 2024 as well with a 41% return, outpacing the S&P 500. ET also pays a quarterly distribution yielding about 6.5%.

The NXG Closed End Funds for Infrastructure Income

As an income-oriented investor, I want to be able to benefit from this growth trajectory based on the increasing demand for power generation in the next decade. One way to do that is by owning shares of NXG NextGen Infrastructure Income Fund (NYSE:NXG). This fund has some similarities to its sibling fund, NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund (SRV), which owns more MLPs and pipeline companies. Both funds share the same portfolio managers, John Musgrave and Todd Sunderland. And both funds substantially increased the distributions in 2023, with NXG doubling the monthly payout starting in September 2023.

I have previously written about SRV several times. My most recent coverage was exactly a year ago on February 24, 2024 when I rated SRV a Buy. SRV delivered a total return of nearly 44% in the year since publication.

Although this is my first detailed review of NXG, I have owned it for quite some time in my personal account and at times have traded shares. For example, I sold shares of NXG at a relatively high premium in November to buy shares of SRV when they did a rights offering, and then later sold some SRV shares for a gain when they traded at a high premium and bought back NXG shares later when they had a price dip in December.

Over the past year, NXG with its higher yield distribution (currently 13.6%) has outperformed SRV.

So, let’s take a deeper dive into NXG. The fund has a fact sheet that is current as of 12/31/24 that I will use snippets from to describe the fund and its key facts.

Why invest in the fund? If you are an income investor who wishes to benefit from the growing energy demand that I described above, this fund offers one option for doing so. Here are some bullet points from the fact sheet to explain other reasons:

• Unique focus on infrastructure for today & tomorrow’s economy (e.g. digital/technological infrastructure) along with traditional infrastructure investments

• Focus on responsible and sustainable investments

• Seeks to invest in uncorrelated growth opportunities that are secular vs. cyclical

• Seeks to invest in companies that have high degree of demand inelasticity

• Designed to take advantage of traditional infrastructure investment characteristics (e.g. lower correlation to broader market, high barriers to entry, business activities that are vital to economy, etc.).

The types of fund investments include clean and sustainable infrastructure like renewables, EV, hydrogen, water, and waste infrastructure; communications and technology infrastructure including data storage, wireline, wireless, cloud, satellite communication, and emerging infrastructure; energy infrastructure including midstream energy, electrical generation, and natural gas distribution; and industrial infrastructure such as toll roads, ports, airports, railroads, etc.

The key facts are shown in this snippet from the fact sheet:

The top ten holdings in NXG are shown on the website and include the three that I mentioned above: ET, VST, and CEG. The other top holdings are midstream companies that are also top holdings in SRV.

As you can see in the snippet below from SA, the top 10 holdings in SRV include overlap with NXG. Both funds hold ET in the number 1 position and Targa Resources (TRGP) in the #3 spot. Both funds also hold Williams Companies (WMB), DT Midstream, MPLX, Kinder Morgan, and Cheniere Energy (LNG).

It is no wonder that NXG has been able to significantly grow the NAV and continue to pay a very high yield distribution since September 2023 with such a strong lineup of high total return contenders. Just out of curiosity, I conducted a portfolio backtest analysis using Portfolio Visualizer to discover what sort of total return would have been realized from a portfolio of just the top ten holdings since September 2023. The result was that a $10,000 investment in those top 10 holdings would be worth nearly $25,000 today, with an annualized return of 87%.

$10,000 invested in September 1, 2023 would be worth $24,289 as of January 31, 2025, which represents a cumulative return of 142.89%. Over the same period, the benchmark would be worth $13,641, which represents a cumulative return of 36.41%. Over the period, the portfolio generated a return of 87.09% per year, with 14 out of 17 or 82.35% of months positive. Over the same period, the benchmark generated a return of 24.50% per year, with 12 out of 17 or 70.59% of months positive.

NXG Distribution History and Managed Distribution Policy

On February 3, 2025, NXG announced a $0.54 per share monthly distribution to be made payable in the months of February, March, April, and May 2025. The fund also estimates that each month’s distribution will consist of 100% ROC (return of capital).

In the footnote at the bottom of the press release is this explanation of the ROC aspect of the tax treatment:

The return of capital estimate is based on the Fund’s current anticipated earnings and profits for the fiscal year and does not include a projection of gains and losses on the sale of securities which may occur during the remainder of the year. It is currently anticipated, but not certain, that approximately 100% of the Fund’s distributions will be treated as a return of capital. The final determination of such amounts will be made and reported to shareholders in 2026, after the end of the calendar year when the Fund determines its earnings and profits for the year. The final tax status of each distribution may differ substantially from this preliminary information.

For 2024, the entire year’s distributions did include 100% ROC as shown on the Form 8937.

This tax treatment of 100% ROC is appealing to investors for its favorable tax implications if the security is held in a taxable account. There is no UBTI to report and no K-1 to deal with at tax time, but an investment in NXG will reduce your cost basis by the amount of the distribution paid each month. That is an attractive aspect of the fund for those investors who benefit from the reduced cost basis in a taxable account. This explanation from Eaton Vance goes into more detail about the pros and cons of ROC in a taxable brokerage account.

A few key takeaways to remember: Return of capital is a tax concept, not an economic concept. Return of capital distributions are not subject to current tax. Compared to ordinary dividends, they offer the potential advantages of reducing (to long-term capital gains rates) and deferring (until year of sale) shareholder taxes. The tax character of a fund’s distributions tells little about whether the distributions are supported by fund returns – dividends and capital gains distributions may be unearned and return of capital distributions can be earned. The best measure of whether a fund has earned its distributions is the change in its NAV net of distributions. Regardless of how distributions are characterized, if a fund’s NAV increases, the fund earned its distribution. If not, the fund did not earn its distribution – the economic concept of return of principal.

Fund NAV and Premium/Discount History

Another advantage to investing in a CEF rather than buying individual MLPs to capture strong total returns is that the market price of a CEF like NXG can vary from a discount below NAV to a premium above NAV. When the market offers a bigger than average discount, that is often a good time to buy shares. And when the premium reaches a higher-than-average level, it may be a good time to trim some shares to capture some capital gains.

The fund website shows the history of the fund discount/premium over the past ten years and you can clearly see that over the past two years the discount has been closing and the fund is starting to trade at a small premium now in early 2025.

That is good news for the fund managers and one of the objectives that they wanted to achieve by doubling the distribution in 2023. By attracting more investors to the fund, they can grow the portfolio, deliver stronger returns and continue to support the distribution. Another method in addition to a rights offering for growing a closed end fund is to offer additional shares in ATM (at-the-market) offerings. On January 28, 2025, NXG announced an ATM offering.

Risks to Future Performance

If the economy comes to a screeching halt for whatever reason, and investor demand for utilities and energy suddenly slow due to some black swan event or major economic disaster that nobody could predict, the fund could falter. We saw some evidence of trepidation in the market on Friday 2/21 when the market dropped precipitously due to concerns over such an economic catastrophe potentially on the horizon due to threats from rising inflation, possible impacts of Trump’s tariffs, or whatever news that caused the rapid selloff.

The price of NXG dropped nearly -2.5% in that one day of trading, which may have been a buying opportunity. The utilities sector has been on a rampage for most of 2024 but it may be levelling off now in 2025. Oil prices have been vacillating but trending lower lately (back to a YTD low), which creates negative sentiment around midstream energy stocks even though they are only indirectly impacted by oil prices.

The momentum may be interrupted in the short-term but long-term investors may still want to buy NXG now and collect a nearly 14% yield from monthly distributions that are declared out through May already. The distribution will most likely remain the same for the remainder of 2025 unless things change dramatically in the next few months.

In conclusion, I believe that NXG is a well-managed CEF that currently offers a high yield distribution of 13.6%. It has the potential for additional capital appreciation over the coming few years as the increasing demand for power generation continues to drive strong total returns in the energy and industrial infrastructure sectors.

data.title: Home Depot: Warnings Signs Are Building As The Company Prepares For Q4 Earnings
data.description:
Sometimes secular moves within an industry are just as important as overall business cycles. No investment goes straight up. While certain industries have longer-term moves, almost all sectors eventually go through good times and bad.

The housing market has been very strong coming out of Covid-19 the shutdowns that followed the epidemic. After the pandemic ended pent-up demand in this industry was strong, people wanted to move from more urban areas that were particularly hard hit to the suburbs, and rates were historically low.

One company that benefitted significantly from the strength in the housing market was The Home Depot, Inc. (NYSE:HD). The retailer was well positioned to take advantage of people working remotely who upgraded their home, an increase in home owners, and the fact that the consumer saving rate was at a historically high when many people were locked in personal residences. Most people also had access to cheap capital because of how low rates were during and coming out of Covid-19 as well.

Home Depot has offered investors total returns of 77.19 percent over the last 5 years, despite selling off recently, while the S&P 500 (SP500) has offered investors total returns of 94.16 percent during the same time period.

I last wrote about Home Depot in February of 2024, and I rated the company a strong sell primarily because I viewed the retailer's earnings cycle as likely peaking. Today, I am reiterating my strong sell rating. Recent economic data suggests the economy is slowing, the housing market looks overpriced, and interest rates are likely to remain elevated since prices are still high.

Home Depot's third-quarter earnings report highlighted the accelerating weakness the company is seeing in the retailer's core business. Home Depot saw the average ticket decline again for the second straight quarter, management reported that comparable sales decreased by 1.2% since last year, and operating margins fell from 14.5% to 13.8% on a year-to-year basis as well. The company reported that earnings per share declined from $3.81 a share, to $3.67 a share on a year-to-year basis. Management also warned that customers were being increasingly cautious.

Home Depot's net margin has now been in decline for seven straight quarters, since January of 2023. The company's 12-month trailing revenue peaked at that time as well.

There are multiple signs that the housing market is slowing down as well. Sales of existing homes fell by 4.9 percent from December to January, construction permits declined by 1.7 percent in January from the same month a year ago, and home builders confidence slipped to a 5-month low. The Fed has also signaled that future rates are likely to occur slowly, with prices remaining high. The average mortgage is nearly $500 a month higher now than in 2022.

Home Depot also currently trades at 25.84x projected forward GAAP earnings even though analysts are projecting the retailer to grow EPS at just a 6-8 percent over the next 6 years, and the company's current earnings cycle is likely coming to an end, with growing signs of an economic slowdown and that housing prices are at unsustainable levels. Companies and governments are also increasingly requiring people to return to work from home, so demand for DIY projects will likely continue to fall as well.

This is why Home Depot's next earnings report for the fourth quarter, coming up on the morning of Tuesday, February 25th, will likely disappoint even slightly muted expectations. While earnings per share and revenue numbers are obviously important, investors should particular of attention to average ticket size, same-store sales numbers, and overall operating margins as well. Recently reported consumer spending numbers in January were very disappointing, there have been a number of companies that have recently announced major layoffs, including JPMorgan (JPM), and Chevron (CVX), and rates and prices remain high. Walmart also recently warned that consumers are being more cautious as well. The retailer has significant downside risk going into earnings on Tuesday.

data.title: S&P 500 Beats A Worried Retreat After Touching New Record High
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SPX) had a pretty active week despite the trading week being shortened by the Presidents Day holiday. The index reached an all-time new record high of 6,144.15 on Wednesday, 19 February 2025 before retreating to close out the week at 6,013.13, about 1.7% below where it ended the week before.

Driving the market to its new record high was a continuation of the momentum in investor expectations toward more rather than fewer Federal Reserve rate cuts in 2025.

That shift could be seen in the CME Group's FedWatch Tool, which is once again forecasting two rate cuts in 2025. It projects a quarter-point rate cut when Fed meets on 18 June (2025-Q2), six weeks later than it predicted a week ago. Meanwhile, the FedWatch tool is also forecasting another quarter point rate cut when the Fed meets on 10 December (2025-Q4).

But late in the week, something prompted investors to suddenly shift their forward-looking attention inward to the nearer-term future of 2025-Q2. The latest update of the alternative futures chart shows that sudden shift, with the trajectory of the index moving to the bottom of the typical range for where we would expect to find it when investors focus their attention on 2025-Q2.

Since the change was less than two percent, we don't have high confidence in a prominent explanation for the sudden drop, which traces back to a report pointing to the possible emergence of a new infectious coronavirus in China. There are competing explanations for the sudden pullback in the S&P 500, which you can evaluate for yourself in the market-moving headlines of the week that was:

Tuesday, 18 February 2025

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • Oil prices extend gains after Ukraine drone strike hits Kazakh supply
      • OPEC+ is not considering delay to April oil supply hike, Novak says
    • Trump says he will introduce 25% tariffs on autos, drugs and chips
      • FACTBOX All of Donald Trump's tariffs and threatened trade actions
  • Fed officials starting to realize they didn't fix inflation, claim they won't become deer in the headlights because of uncertainty, but still plan to keep U.S. interest rates unchanged:
    • Fed policy should stay restrictive until there's more inflation progress: Daly
    • Fed's Waller: Uncertainty over trade, other policies, shouldn't paralyze rate moves
      • Fed's Bowman: Greater confidence in falling inflation needed before more cuts
    • Fed's Harker supports steady interest rate policy stance for now
  • Bigger stimulus developing in China:
    • The significance of China Xi's meet with private enterprises
      • China's tech rally rests on 'hot money'
  • BOJ officials expected to hike Japan's interest rates after claiming they will:
    • Japan braces for BOJ to lift rates sooner and higher
    • Japan's GDP beats forecasts as consumption, business spending perk up
  • Other central banks cutting interest rates, becoming world's biggest gold bugs:
    • Australia's central bank cuts rates, cautious on further easing
    • Goldman Sachs raises gold forecast citing strong central bank demand
  • ECB officials excited to keep working from home where nobody can see how much they really do:
    • ECB extends work from home policy even as others reverse course
  • S&P 500 ends at record high as 'overvalued' stock market dashes up in last minutes of trade
    • S&P 500 ekes out all-time closing high as Fed minutes eyed
    • Trump sending fewer market moving social media posts than previous term, study shows

Wednesday, 19 February 2025

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • Oil holds near one-week high on supply concerns, sanctions on Russia eyed
      • S crude stockpiles likely rose last week, products seen down
    • GM CFO says if US tariffs are permanent, company must consider plant locations
  • Fed officials want to see more progress on their fixing inflation before cutting US interest rates again:
    • Fed officials seek ‘further progress’ on inflation before cutting rates
      • Fed officials weighed slowing or pausing bond drawdown last month
      • Potential impact of Trump policies stirring inflation concerns at Fed, minutes show
  • Bigger trouble, stimulus developing in China:
    • China's new home prices stagnate in January as demand struggles
    • In balancing risks and rewards, China set to leave lending rates unchanged in Feb
  • Another central bank acts to cut interest rates:
    • New Zealand central bank cuts rates by 50bps
  • ECB officials arguing policy amongst themselves
    • Top ECB policymakers outline opposing policy arguments
    • ECB's Panetta says Europe should consider simpler banking rules
  • Wall Street notches another record close after Fed minutes, tariff action
    • S&P 500 edges to record closing high as Fed minutes parsed

Thursday, 20 February 2025

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • Oil rises for third day on US fuel stocks draw, worries about Russia disruptions
    • US new vehicle sales set to rise 8.1% in February, report says
    • US labor market stable; hit from federal government belt tightening awaited
  • Fed officials want to see more progress on their fixing inflation before cutting US interest rates again:
    • Fed's Musalem flags risk of rising inflation expectations and stagflation
    • Fed's Goolsbee says PCE inflation reading unlikely to be as 'sobering' as CPI
    • Fed's Jefferson says Fed can take time on next interest rate decision
    • Fed's Barr, stepping down from regulatory post, warns against weaker bank rules
  • Bigger steel prices developing in China:
    • Iron ore gains on strong China steel consumption, short covering
  • BOJ officials expected to hike interest rates again as Japan's inflation risk rises:
    • BOJ to raise rates once more this year to 0.75%, most likely in Q3
    • BOJ may raise rates twice more this year, underpin yen, says ex-FX diplomat Watanabe
  • ECB officials running a money-losing business:
    • ECB reports another record annual loss, says it may suffer more losses
  • Stocks finish lower, with Dow down 1% as Walmart guidance falls short
    • Wall St slides as weak Walmart guidance prompts worries about consumer demand
      • Higher egg prices drove up inflation, and Walmart grocery revenue
    • Gold prices soar to record high fueled by safe-haven demand

Friday, 21 February 2025

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • US existing home sales fall more than expected in January
      • US Existing-Home Sales Drop Back Again With Mortgage Rates at 7%
    • Oil falls 2% but still set for slight weekly gain on supply jitters
    • DOJ launches UnitedHealth billing probe, WSJ reports; insurer says it is unaware
  • Fed officials blame AI for faster investor reactions to Fed official statements, continue to worry about untamed inflation:
    • Fed's Jefferson says AI is speeding investors' reactions to central bankers' messages
    • Fed's Kugler says inflation risks remain, cites economic policy uncertainty
  • Bigger trouble, failing stimulus developing in China:
    • Another pandemic scare as scientists discover new coronavirus in China
    • China's key property rescue tools are too hard to use to make an impact
  • BOJ officials thinking about starting spring with a rate hike as Japan's inflation rears up:
    • BOJ could raise interest rates in March, says ex-policymaker Shirai
    • Japan’s core inflation hits 19-month high
      • BOJ isn't fretting much about rising bond yields - for now
    • Japan's factory activity shrinks for 8th straight month, PMI shows
  • Nasdaq ends 2% lower as gloomy economic data fuel U.S. stocks to weekly losses
    • China Reports New Coronavirus 'With Pandemic Potential' Discovered
    • UnitedHealth sends Medicare insurers lower after report on DoJ probe

The Atlanta Fed's GDPNow tool's projection of what real GDP growth will be in the 2025-Q1 held steady at +2.3% for a second week.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Kemira: Long-Term Growth Potential Remains Intact
data.description:
Introduction

Kemira (OTCPK:KOYJF) has released its financial results for 2024, and those results were pretty flat as there were no surprises (nor positive nor negative). I originally liked the company for its relatively defensive characteristics, as this specialty chemicals company is very active in the water treatment industry. The exposure to water solutions and water treatment is the main reason why I’m interested in this Finnish company.

The company sold its oil & gas division last year and this immediately improved the balance sheet as the net debt ratio dropped to less than 0.3 times EBITDA.

Kemira has its main listing on the Helsinki Stock Exchange, where it's trading with KEMIRA as ticker symbol. The average daily volume in Finland is approximately 180,000 shares per day, making it the most liquid venue to trade in the company’s shares. I will use the Euro as base currency throughout this article. The current share count is approximately 154 million shares, which results in a market capitalization of approximately 3.34B EUR.

The Cash Flow Is Stronger Than The Net Income

In the final quarter of the year, the company reported a total revenue of approximately 724M EUR, which is a decrease of approximately 10% on a reported basis but keep in mind the Q4 2023 revenue still included the contribution from the oil & gas division. Excluding that division, the underlying revenue actually increased by approximately 5%.

This resulted in an EBITDA of approximately 116.5M EUR and an EBIT of 67M EUR. The net finance expenses were pretty low and this enabled Kemira to report a pre-tax income of almost 62M EUR which yielded 51.1M EUR in net profit. Approximately 3M EUR of the net income was attributable to non-controlling interests, which means Kemira’s own net profit came in at 48M EUR, which represents an EPS of 0.31 EUR.

Looking at the full-year result, Kemira reported a total revenue of 2.95B EUR, which is approximately 10% lower than the revenue reported in 2023. The EBITDA increased by approximately 2% despite having sold the oil & gas division. Looking at the bottom-line result, the 249M EUR in net attributable profit represented an EPS of 1.62 EUR per share.

Looking at the full-year cash flow result, Kemira reported an operating cash flow of just under 485M EUR, but this included a cash tax payment of 89.6M EUR, although only 74M EUR in taxes was owed, as per the income statement. The reported operating cash flow also includes a 28.5M EUR contribution from working capital changes and excludes the almost 32M EUR in lease payments. This means the underlying operating cash flow was approximately 440M EUR on an underlying and normalized basis.

The total capex incurred in 2024 was just 167M EUR, which results in an adjusted free cash flow of 273M EUR. This represents an FCFPS of approximately 1.77 EUR per share.

That being said, as the image below shows, only 106M EUR of the entire capex was classified as maintenance capex with an additional 42M EUR in improvement investments and 20M EUR in growth.

Even if I’d only exclude the growth investments from the equation, the underlying free cash flow result per share would have been around 1.90 EUR. And if we would assume a portion of the improvement capex should be seen as growth capex rather than pure maintenance capex, the net free cash flow per share goes up to around 2 EUR per share.

At the end of 2024, Kemira had approximately 519M EUR in cash on the balance sheet, while the net debt was just 291M EUR (and about half of that net debt consisted of lease liabilities). This means the debt ratio was just around 0.2-0.3 times the lease-adjusted EBITDA. Meanwhile, at the current share price, Kemira is trading at an EV/EBITDA multiple of approximately 6.75 which is very reasonable.

The Expectations For 2025

The company also already provided an outlook for 2025, and Kemira expects to generate a revenue of 2.8-3.2B EUR and the midpoint of this guidance range represents a 1.5% increase compared to the FY 2024 revenue.

Kemira also published an operative EBITDA guidance of 540-640M EUR and the midpoint of 590M EUR represents a 1% increase compared to the 2024 result. In a way, the 2025 guidance is perhaps a bit disappointing, but fortunately the company will continue to generate plenty of cash flow which it can reinvest in itself.

Kemira also announced a dividend of 0.74 EUR per share based on the FY 2024 results and considering the payout ratio remains low, I think it’s fair to assume the dividend will increase in FY 2025 as well, even if the results remain flat. Unfortunately, Finnish dividends are subject to a 35% dividend withholding tax (investors should consult with a tax specialist to see if and how (a portion of) the Finnish tax can be recovered).

Investment Thesis

While the midpoint of this year’s guidance is ‘uninspiring’, Kemira may perhaps surprise to the upside as its revenue and EBITDA ranges are pretty wide. In any case, the company’s net debt should continue to decrease, and I would expect Kemira to be net debt free by the end of 2026.

I sold my position in Kemira in 2024, but I may have sold too soon, as Kemira’s results are holding up pretty well. I would consider going long again, but I am in no rush.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 4 Macro Scenarios Holding Me Back From TLT (For Now)
data.description:
The iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (NASDAQ:TLT) was launched by iShares in 2002. As a low-cost, highly liquid fund of US Treasury Bonds, I believe its original intent was to attract investors who wanted to limit volatility in their portfolios.

The problem for anyone who used TLT as part of a defensive 60 / 40 portfolio is that they did not account for the new territories the FED’s monetary policy would enter between 2010 and 2021. As Zero-Interest-Rates-Policy (ZIRP) became the “new norm,” yields on all forms of US debt naturally started to decline over time. Initially, TLT appreciated as an effect of ZIRP - moving from a price in the 90s before 2008 to the 120s from 2012 onwards.

However, after the FED’s hawkish pivot in 2022 brought an end to ZIRP, equities and bonds both saw a significant correction. This was not an issue for investors holding actual US Treasury Bonds (as they could simply wait expiration to recover their capital), but it was (and still is) for those who chose TLT.

TLT, as a passive fund with a rolling portfolio of bonds, does not reimburse its investors of their “principals.” Investors who bought TLT during the ZIRP era saw a significant correction and now have to hope for a return of zero interest rates to recover their capital.

In this regard, TLT has become an odd product. I would not recommend its use to anyone looking for broad exposure to US Treasuries. However, it is perfect to gamble on the direction of interest rates. Even the web page of TLT just vaguely states its objective as “Use to customize your exposure to Treasuries.”

In this article, I present four different macro scenarios concerning the future direction of US interest rates, and particularly the expected US long term “equilibrium interest rate,” or R*, which will ultimately determine TLT’s performance.

TLT is a bet on Macroeconomics: What is R*?

“R*” is the macroeconomic symbol indicating equilibrium interest rate. The equilibrium interest rate is the theoretical real interest rate where the economy operates at full employment and stable inflation, neither stimulating nor slowing growth.

Because TLT is so heavily influenced by interest rates, its performance (and investment thesis) ultimately depends on what is the long term equilibrium interest rate for the US.

The reason why I am focusing on equilibrium interest rates rather than actual interest rates set by the FED is two-fold. First, because provided that an investor has a long enough time horizon, R* is what actually matters in the context of betting on TLT; they can expect interest rates to revert to the equilibrium, eventually.

Second, because I find it makes more sense to reason long-term rather than short-term when it comes to macroeconomics. I believe that long-term macro trends are easier to forecast. In the short term, the vast majority of macro prediction, including those about interest rates, fail (something I will cover in my risk section).

R* in the US has been 0% to 1% for the vast majority of the last 15 years. These were the times of Zero-Interest-Rate-Policy following the 2008 crisis. Historically however, the R* for the US economy was always in the 2% to 4% range before 2008. This was also the case in the 1970s, despite the FED having raised rates in the double digits at times to bring inflation down.

The real question when it comes to TLT, then, is: what is the R* for the US economy, today? Is ZIRP the “new norm” to revert back to, or is a 2% to 4% range to be expected again?

In the next paragraphs, I present two bullish and two bearish short- and mid-term scenarios with four different outcomes in terms of R*.

TLT Bullish scenario #1: inflation under control

My first bullish scenario would play out as follows:

  • US Inflation shows a decisive, declining pace in the upcoming CPI readings

  • As a result, the FED signals 1 to 3 interest rate cuts in 2025 and early 2026

  • Inflation goes back permanently to a sub-2% level, and the long term R* turns out to be between 2% to 3% (reversion to historical norm).

  • Disinflationary policies from the current US admin that may play a role in this scenario are significant cuts in government spending (e.g., as effect of DOGE) or a sudden decrease in oil prices (e.g., as effect of the war in Ukraine coming to an end).

In this scenario, I would expect TLT to have a positive correlation with equity markets. I believe both TLT and generally equities would experience a bullish run, should inflation go back under control relatively quickly and interest rates decline relatively fast.

As a result of this expected positive correlation, I am not particularly interested in investing in TLT at current prices based on this scenario. I am already heavily invested in equities and risk assets such as Bitcoin (BTC-USD), and I am not interested in a relatively modest upside from TLT when I can expect more from other parts of my portfolio.

My expectation for this scenario would be for TLT to reach levels last seen in September 2024 - when inflation was seemingly under control and the uncertainty surrounding a Trump’s election victory wasn’t priced in. My target price here is ~$100 and represents a ~12% upside from TLT’s prices at the time of writing.

TLT Bullish scenario #2: rising inflation triggers a deep recession

My second bullish scenario for TLT would play out as follows:

  • US Inflation starts rising in the next CPI readings (consistent 3%+ figures), indicating it may be re-accelerating well above the FED’s 2% yearly target.

  • As a result, the FED indicates no rate cuts at all for 2025, and/or hints at possible rate hikes in 2025 and 2026, depending on exact inflation trends.

  • Due to the FED actions and overall economic climate, the US enters a deep recession in late 2025 or early 2026.

  • As a result of the recession, inflation craters well below the FED’s 2% target and the FED immediately pivots to sub-2% rates to stimulate the economy. The FED will eventually revert back to ZIRP, meaning R* would fall below 1%.

  • Inflationary policies from the current admin that may play a role in this scenario are significant US trade tariffs (e.g., on the EU, Canada or Mexico) and mass deportations (tightening the job market) igniting inflation in the short-term.

In this scenario, I would expect TLT to decline significantly in the short-term to then significantly bounce once the FED pivots to sub-2% interest rates. It would be the absolute most bullish scenario for TLT if the FED indicated a permanent return to ZIRP, as before the pandemic.

My target price for TLT in this scenario would be in the $ 130 - $ 170 range, assuming a reversion to R* below 1%. These were the levels last seen in 2020, well before post pandemic inflation manifested itself and when the markets were expecting ZIRP to be the new norm for the foreseeable future.

In this scenario, I would most likely get to buy TLT at a discount against current prices - as initial inflation readings and FED pivots to higher rates would punish the fund. This is why I am also not interested in entering a trade on TLT at current prices, based on this bullish scenario. However, should it manifest in the upcoming months, I might revise my recommendation to a BUY (more on this in my conclusions).

TLT Bearish scenario #1: stagflation and Volkerism 2.0

The most worrying scenario for TLT would be that of stagflation. This could manifest as follows:

  • US Inflation starts rising in the next CPI readings (consistent 3%+ figures), indicating it may be re-accelerating well above the FED’s 2% yearly target.

  • As a result, the FED indicates no rate cuts at all for 2025, and/or hints at possible rate hikes in 2025 and 2026, depending on exact inflation trends.

  • Despite the FED’s actions, a US recession does not manifest, or it does but without bringing inflation down.

  • The FED is now stuck between accepting higher inflation as the new norm, or significantly raising rates to hurt the economy (something reminiscent of Volkerism). In either case, I would expect rates to be higher for longer, and R* to stabilize slightly above the historical norm, in the 3% to 4% range.

  • Politics may play a role here should the FED work against the government - for example rising rates while the government hands out money in various forms to appease citizens (e.g., the rumor that Americans will receive handouts from the savings generated by DOGE).

In this scenario, I would once again expect TLT to have a positive correlation with stocks. Both would experience a prolonged bear market, in my view, should a stagflation scenario become reality. This is once again the reason why I am not interested in entering a TLT trade at this moment in time. I would be only if I expected TLT to de-risk my equity-heavy portfolio, which isn’t the case.

In this scenario, I would expect TLT to correct to eventually revert to the same levels of today - pricing in a long term R* of 3% to 4%. Needless to say, this scenario does not represent a compelling investment proposition in TLT at current levels.

TLT Bearish scenario #2: Black Swan event significantly raises R*

My last bearish scenario for TLT is also the least likely to happen, as it concerns a possible black swan event affecting key aspects of the US economy. Ultimately, the equilibrium interest rate depends on many macroeconomic factors, such as the productivity, demographics and savings behavior of actors in an economy.

Should any of these factors be heavily influenced, I would expect R* to move in unexpected ways - potentially stabilizing well above the historical norm, at 5% or more. I will provide two examples of what may trigger such an event.

First, is Artificial Intelligence. AI may very well produce an unexpected, major increase in labor productivity. Should this happen, the US economy may “overheat” to a point that the equilibrium interest rate needed to keep inflation at 2% could become 5% or higher. This scenario would manifest

The second scenario is that of a deadlock between the government and the FED, where the FED is eventually strong handed into accepting 3% as the new inflation target. Should this happen, I would expect R* to stabilize at the very least in a range from 4% to 5%.

A significant raise in R* for the US economy will be very bearish for TLT, and would most likely result in the fund correcting permanently to lower price levels than today.

Conclusion: my strategy to play TLT

I do not believe a compelling thesis exists today to enter a trade in TLT:

  1. If inflation comes back quickly under control, I would expect equities to perform as well if not better than this fund from current prices.

  2. If there is a deep recession, TLT may overperform equities in the mid- and long-term assuming ZIRP comes back, but it will first correct from current levels, representing a better entry point.

  3. If the US enters a stagflationary period and eventually reverts back to the historical norm of 2% to 3% equilibrium interest rates, TLT is already fairly priced.

  4. A black swan event permanently raising US equilibrium interest rates to 5%+ (for example, a sudden increase in labor productivity because of AI) is the most bearish scenario for TLT.

My strategy on TLT for now is to observe. Should the US enter a recession, I will revise my recommendation and - if inflation data signals stagflation is NOT likely - I may enter TLT at a discount against current prices. If I were to enter TLT, I would do so via Long Call options (probably LEAPS), to maximize my upside.

Should inflation come back under control in the next months, I will be OK having “missed out” on TLT, as I expect a relatively limited upside. I would benefit from this scenario in any case, being heavily exposed to other risk assets such as equities.

Risks

Overall, the main element of risk with entering any trade in TLT is that it is historically very hard to predict the direction of interest rates. As the chart above shows, the market is consistently wrong about interest rates, and it tends to underestimate or overestimate any movement in either direction. That's why I have focused my article on R* rather than actual interest rates.

The market is currently expecting one or no interest rate cut in 2025, and there are discussions of possible stagflation. If I had to place a bet based on how wrong the market has historically been, I would say neither scenario is likely. I would rather bet on a slow disinflationary trend, with inflation eventually coming back under control by the end of year. Today, I would also still bet that ZIRP will eventually make a comeback. My belief is rooted in global ageing demographics and the overall impoverishment of the Western middle class - a secular, deflationary trend that I don’t see reversing in the future.

Another risk worth noting with my thesis concerns the specifics of my second bearish scenario. A black swan labor productivity event may end up playing in favor of TLT, contrary to my assumptions. For example, mass layoffs due to AI replacing human input in the job market may more than compensate for increases in labor productivity due to AI. In the context of consumer inflation, wage growth counts far more than productivity and this may be disrupted as a result of AI adoption. However, for the sake of this exercise, I have only assumed a possible negative impact on R* for my scenario.

Finally, there is also a risk in focusing on R*, instead of actual interest rates. Ultimately, R* is a theoretical concept that is never officially declared by the FED, but only inferred by economists. Regardless of what the R* of the US economy is, actual rates may stay significantly above or below it for long periods of time. An example is the 1970s, where rates stayed well above the 2 to 3% theoretical R* for more than a decade.

data.title: Compass Therapeutics: Good Execution, Major Catalysts Ahead
data.description:
I covered Compass Therapeutics (NASDAQ:CMPX), a Boston company with positive data from a South Korean trial, back in August 2023. The data is from South Korea because lead candidate CTX-009, a dual DLL4 x VEGF-A inhibitor, was developed by ABL Bio of South Korea. This molecule, also called tovecimig, targets 2nd line biliary tract cancer. It is running a phase 3 trial, with topline data expected in Q1 2025.

Tovecimig also completed a phase 1/2 monotherapy trial in 3L/4L colorectal cancer back in 2023. One more trial in 2L CRC without chemo will begin this year. Besides tovecimig, there is CTX-471, which completed a basket study in post-checkpoint inhibitor solid tumors, and will begin another phase 2 basket study in NCAM(CD56)-positive cancers. Another molecule, CTX-8371, completed dose escalation and will produce phase 1 data in the second half of 2025. Two other molecules are in preclinical stages.

In that 2023 article, I discussed the Korean data. I will not go into those details now - you can read them here - but I will only note my impression. The data was all over the place. There were a number of nuances in the data:

  • Monotherapy safety profile was good, with no DLTs and the only grade 3 AE being hypertension (2021 data)
  • Combo therapy with paclitaxel had a poorer safety profile, with one death through pneumonia and multiple TEAEs causing discontinuations
  • The above safety data did not compare well with other BTC chemo trials
  • In the monotherapy basket trial, CRC was the most successful indication, however, in general, ORR was not good, while DCR was strong, meaning the drug worked better at the lower end of the disease burden spectrum
  • This was also seen with prior VEGF patients, which improved results in the lower end, with PD and SD patients
  • DLL4+ patients did very well, considering the DLL4 inhibiting nature of the molecule. Gastric patients with DLL4 did the best
  • However, that conclusion is skewed by the fact that there were too few DLL4- patients to compare with

So this is the data - I hope I have been able to give you the impression that the data is scattered and all over the place - because that is the impression I get. The molecule works, but where it works, in what situation and under what circumstances - these things need to be brought out of this morass of data. I hope that will happen with the currently running trial.

This is the COMPANION-02 trial which has a primary completion date of July 2025. This is an US trial, an open-label, randomized, phase 2/3 trial combining chemotherapy paclitaxel versus paclitaxel alone in patients “with unresectable advanced, metastatic or recurrent biliary tract cancers who have received one prior systemic chemotherapy regimen.” Total estimated enrollment is 150 patients - enrollment was completed in August 2024. The primary endpoint is overall response rate (ORR') and secondary endpoints include progression free survival (PFS), overall survival (OS), clinical benefit rate (CBR), and duration of response (DOR).

This trial, I hope, will bring out the right sort of data in the cancer they selected, BTC. Now, the annual incidence rate of BTC in the US is about 8000. 40% of these are intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma. BTC is a relatively rare cancer, accounting for just 1% of all cancers. The 5-year survival rate of metastatic BTC is just 5%. The market size is small, projected to be just about $1.4bn by 2030.

A number of treatment paradigms are employed. Because the cancer is often detected in metastatic stages, chemotherapy is standardly used. Targeted and immunotherapy drugs include Pemazyre (pemigatinib) from Incyte Corporation (INCY), Imfinzi (durvalumab) from AstraZeneca (AZN), and the very recently approved Ziihera (zanidatamab) from Jazz Pharmaceuticals (JAZZ). To be noted, ORR rate was 52% from the registrational trial that led to approval for the Jazz drug. This is an accelerated approval, and there was a 53% rate of AEs in the trial. Those are the benchmarks for tovecimig.

Among pipeline drugs are a number of molecules from Asian biopharmas like Eisai, Taiho, BeiGene and so on.

Financials

CMPX has a market cap of $423mn and a cash balance of $135mn as of the last reported quarter. R&D expenses were $8.6mn and G&A expenses were $3.6mn. At that rate, they have a cash runway of 10 quarters.

Looking at this from an FCF perspective, their CFO was ($11.1) and CapEx was zero, therefore their FCF was ($11.1). Dividing the cash balance with this, we get a cash runway of 11 quarters.

The company filed a mixed shelf offering for $300mn, however they have not actually diluted yet.

Risks

BTC is a difficult cancer with poor success rates. Moreover, there are a few approved therapies, including the latest from Jazz. Although zanidatamab targets HER2+ BTC while tovecimig targets DLL4+ BTC, one should keep in mind that this is a relatively small market. Other than they, they are sufficiently funded, and there is a major upcoming catalyst.

Also note, CMPX has a pretty low trading volume of just around 31,000, although average volume is good at 1.38mn.

Bottomline

The stock more than doubled after this news announcement on Jan 7 about plans to initiate a trial of CTX-471 in patients with tumors expressing neural cell adhesion molecule, or NCAM/CD56. I haven’t touched on this because it sounded inconsequential at the moment. However, the market clearly took notice. What the company said about the prior data for this molecule is this:

Presented CTX-471 clinical data at multiple scientific meetings in 2024, including data demonstrating durable clinical responses in Phase 1 presented at the American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) Annual Meeting and data showing a correlation between levels of NCAM (CD56) expression and disease control presented at the 39th Society for Immunotherapy of Cancer 2024 Annual Meeting.

So anyway, the stock is up a lot on this announcement, and we have a major trial data drop in a few months. I would want the price to settle down a bit before moving in with an investment. But overall, CMPX looks like they are doing good medicine.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cisco Faces Growing Competition For The Hyperscalers
data.description:
Cisco Systems, Inc. (NASDAQ:CSCO) reported a strong turnaround to revenue growth in fiscal Q2 ’25 as the decline in networking sales softened. Management noted in the Q2 ’25 earnings call that customer orders grew by 40% in 1h25, potentially suggesting that customers have worked down their inventory levels and are returning to purchasing new networking equipment.

With the modestly optimistic outlook for e2h25, I’m forecasting Cisco to close eFY25 with net revenue of $59.4b and an adjusted EPS of $3.70/share, a 6% improvement to the top-line and a slight compression at the bottom. Despite these factors, I believe Cisco should realize improved growth in networking equipment sales in the coming years as enterprises turn from AI training to bringing AI applications into the production environment.

At the valuation level, CSCO shares have been experiencing significant expansion to its trading premium, potentially suggesting that investors are valuing CSCO for its AI growth capabilities. With these factors in mind, I am reiterating my BUY rating on CSCO shares with a price target of $70.63/share at 12.75x eFY26 EV/aEBITDA.

Cisco Systems Operations

Cisco realized a strong Q2’25 with new orders growing 29%. AI infrastructure orders by the webscalers grew to $350mm in q2’25 and have cumulated to $700mm YTD, bringing Cisco on track to achieving $1b in eFY25.

Data center switching drove growth as the 800G Nexus switches became more widely adopted. Accordingly, enterprises began ordering AI products like Nexus switches and Unified Computing Systems (UCS) as AI applications move to the production environment.

This is one major area where Arista Networks (ANET) competes with Cisco. Though Cisco’s FY24 networking segment is roughly 5x the size of Arista’s products revenue, Arista is realizing significant growth in the data center space, especially with server interconnects and data center switches. Where Cisco may have experienced a high level of inventory digestion and purchasing fatigue, Arista realized significant growth.

Enterprise spending may continue to grow throughout the duration of eFY25 as more enterprises move from proof-of-concept to production applications with GenAI and AI agents. This shift may drive more campus networking and more spend on data center networking equipment.

Enterprise growth drove new networking product orders for switches, routers, webscale infrastructure, and IoT. Campus switching orders also grew by double digits as Cisco rolls out WiFi 7 access points, primarily driven by the return-to-office phenomenon.

One big paradigm shift worth considering is the transition from AI training to inferencing. I suspect the majority of AI training is being done with servers rented through the cloud service providers ((CSPs)), allowing for compute capacity to be scaled to suit the needs of the training models. I anticipate that much of this will transition to in-house data centers as enterprises transition to bringing their AI applications into production. Given the complexity of AI infrastructure, I believe enterprises will purchase rack-scale solutions that have Cisco’s Hyperfabric and AI pids integrated into the rack. This can potentially be a major growth driver for Cisco in e2h25 and eFY26.

Cisco is also reinventing how businesses can leverage AI applications. In q2’25, Cisco released its new data center Smart Switch and Agile Services Networking. The Smart Switch includes Cisco Silicon One E100 networking chips, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) Pensando DPUs with Cisco Hypershield embedded in the switch. Accordingly, the N9300 Smart Switch will begin shipments in spring 2025 with higher-performance models being first available in 2025.

Industrial IoT also drove significant growth as customers prepare for the deployment of AI-powered robotics and industrial security. Though Cisco does not break out the financial impact of industrial IoT, this sector could be a major growth driver for the company as more manufacturing is brought back to the US and automated. One of Cisco’s competitors in the space is Globalstar (GSAT), a firm that specializes in 5G satellite connectivity for factory automation.

I believe another major area of growth for Cisco will be AI security. One of the biggest concerns in working with external AI models is data leakage, a major threat to private data. For example, DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) was found to have a major data leakage, exposing millions of sensitive records on January 29, 2025. According to Wiz Research, the data leak was the result of a misconfigured cloud storage instance. Though the vulnerability was likely brought forth as a result of the heightened scrutiny of DeepSeek given its geographical origins, I suspect DeepSeek is not alone in posing risks as it relates to data security.

Cisco has made a number of AI-oriented acquisitions in the last year with a primary lean towards security. This includes Robust Intelligence and DeepFactor, both in August 2024, and SnapAttack in January 2025. Cisco also acquired Deeper Insights AI in October 2024 to bring in custom-designed AI solutions for enterprises. Management noted in the q2’25 earnings call that they will be focusing on strategically targeting AI companies to strengthen its position in AI. This can potentially lead to Cisco becoming a competing secured-network AI building platform, similar to Cloudflare (NET).

Cisco Systems Financial Position

Cisco grew its total ARR by 22% to $30.1b in q2’25 with subscription revenue now accounting for 56% of total revenue. This transition is working to Cisco’s benefit as it may shed more light into revenue going forward.

Looking to Q3 ’25, I’m forecasting Cisco to generate $13.9b in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $0.91/share. For eFY25, I’m forecasting revenue to remain strong as customers turn from inventory digestion to purchasing, driving total revenue to grow by 6%. I’m forecasting eFY25 adjusted EPS to come in at $3.70/share as a result of slight compression at the bottom line resulting from the debt issued in Q3 ’24.

Cisco ended Q2 ’25 with $16.85b in cash & equivalents and a total debt position of $31b for a net debt/aEBITDA of 0.66x.

Risks Related To Cisco Systems

Bull Case

Cisco is expanding its offerings with embedded AI and security to enhance its capabilities for enterprises and CSPs. Cisco may be moving into the next networking refresh cycle, potentially driving campus and data center networking sales growth. The firm is also expanding its AI footprint through M&A, potentially creating a secure network environment for AI builders, similar to Cloudflare’s offerings.

Bear Case

Cisco may experience competitive pressure from Arista Networks as the firm deepens its partnerships with the CSPs, potentially leading to slower growth in its networking segment. Cisco may also face some margin compression from tariffs placed on infrastructure, potentially leading to some margin compression in the coming quarters.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

Management authorized a $15b share repurchase program in q2’25. The share repurchase authorization is in addition to Cisco’s remaining $2b, making the total authorization sum to $17b. Cisco also increased the quarterly dividend rate by $0.01/share to $0.41/share.

On a company comparison basis, CSCO shares trade at a slight discount to its peers in the IT infrastructure and networking industries. Looking to CSCO’s performance throughout 2024-to-date, CSCO’s trading premium has significantly expanded from a low of 10.20x to 18.68x EV/EBITDA on an unadjusted basis. This may mean that CSCO shares are either very expensive on a relative basis or the market is pricing CSCO shares higher to meet the more AI-oriented infrastructure companies.

Using an internal valuation model based on my EBITDA forecast and CSCO shares’ historical trading premiums, I believe shares should be priced using my blue-sky scenario at $70.63/share at 12.75x eFY26 EV/aEBITDA. My rationale behind this is that CSCO shares are more consistently trading at a higher premium, suggesting that the historical premiums may not be suitable for valuing the firm. Given these factors, I am reiterating my BUY rating for CSCO shares.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative marketcap and relative multiple.

data.title: JD: Earnings To Reflect Q4 Stimulus
data.description:
JD.com (NASDAQ:JD) is due to report Q4 earnings on March 6th. This article looks at why record revenues are expected, and the outlook for the stock going forward.

Context

JD, like many Chinese stocks, bottomed in 2024 and broke out of its downtrend in September 2024 following the announcement of fiscal and monetary stimulus packages.

While this was a convincing break-out, it wasn't driven by anything specific to JD such as earnings. Broader Chinese indices like the Hang Seng also broke out and JD's e-commerce peers Alibaba (BABA) and PDD Holdings (PDD) experienced very similar moves.

One thing to note in the chart above is that both JD and PDD remain below their October high. BABA, on the other hand, has doubled in 2025 and is far above its October high. Last week, the company released impressive earnings with an EPS beat of nearly 10%. Since the stimulus measures were first announced in September, these earnings were the first proper test of their success. It bodes well for JD on March 6th.

Earnings

JD has been posting solid profits despite the Chinese economic slowdown.

The company generated around $4.7B in free cash flow in the first nine months of 2024. This was mostly used for stock buybacks:

The Company repurchased a total of approximately 255.3 million Class A ordinary shares (equivalent of 127.6 million ADSs) for a total of approximately US$3.6 billion during the nine months ended September 30, 2024.

It also means the dividend of $0.76 should be safe.

Here are the expected numbers for Q4.

These are optimistic estimates. There were 12 revisions higher and revenue of $45.8B would be a record.

This isn't necessarily overly optimistic as the general trend for earnings has been higher. Furthermore, BABA just reported record sales.

However, there could be a high bar set for a positive surprise, and good news will be partially priced in.

Valuation

JD has a PE ratio of 13.48, which is in line with PDD. It was also similar to BABA's valuation until BABA surged higher this year.

BABA and JD are different companies, but the chart above illustrates there is scope for multiple expansion in the e-commerce sector. JD's price-to-sales is very low and it's fair to say the company is undervalued.

That begs the question, why?

The Macro

As JD's annual report underlines, "The majority of our net revenues are derived from retail sales in China." Its success hinges on the Chinese consumer.

Most investors will already be aware of the problems facing the Chinese economy. Deleveraging and the popping of the real estate bubble may have been necessary for long-term prosperity, but it created multiple near-term challenges and left a large part of the population in negative equity. Consumer sentiment is in the doldrums.

To make matters worse, the US has imposed tariffs on Chinese exports and could increase them further if another trade war unfolds. JD's valuation is low because of these headwinds and risks.

The Chinese government and the PBOC ramped up stimulus in September with rate cuts and tools to support stock markets. Further pro-growth measures were introduced throughout Q4 and could boost JD's earnings on March 6th.

It may be beneficial that most of JD's sales are in mainland China and it does not rely on exports to the US. The trade war should have little direct impact other than weighing on the economy and if Beijing keeps ramping up stimulus to counteract the tariffs, demand should be healthy. In other words, JD could reap the positives without being directly impacted by the negatives.

Risks

Optimistic earnings estimates could lead to a miss and a negative reaction.

The longer-term risk comes from a continued slowdown in the Chinese economy and the failure of stimulus. An escalation in the trade war with the US is a major macro risk.

The annual report outlines several risks to doing business in China.

Conclusions

JD will report earnings on March 6th and record revenues are expected due to continued growth and the stimulus measures announced last September. This will be the first real test of these measures.

Optimism seems merited, and BABA's earnings and large rally this year have shown there is room to grow in this segment. JD has an attractive valuation and is well-positioned for gains should some of the gloom lift from the Chinese economy in 2025.
